exam_overview.calculator_policy: Graphing calculator required for Section I Part B and Section II Part A. TI-84 Plus and TI-Nspire CAS are commonly allowed. Calculators NOT permitted for Section I Part A and Section II Part B.
tips: No formula sheet is provided — you must memorize all derivative rules, integral rules, and theorem statements cold.
Show all work on FRQs even when using a calculator; answers without supporting work may receive no credit.
On FRQs, justify conclusions using correct calculus language: reference the specific derivative and its sign, not vague descriptions.
Epsilon-delta proofs are NOT assessed on the AB exam.
Integration by parts, partial fractions, improper integrals, Euler's method, and logistic models are BC-only topics — do not spend time on them for AB.
The AP exam tests three main skills: implementing procedures (~60%), connecting representations (~23%), and justification (~15%).

units.name: Limits and Continuity
key_topics: Algebraic evaluation of limits (factoring, conjugates, trig identities)
Types of discontinuity: removable, jump, infinite
Three-condition definition of continuity at a point
Limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes
key_formulas: Limit exists iff left-hand limit equals right-hand limit
Continuity at c: f(c) exists, lim_{x→c} f(x) exists, and they are equal
Squeeze Theorem: g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) and both outer limits equal L → lim f(x) = L
IVT: f continuous on [a,b], d between f(a) and f(b) → ∃c ∈ (a,b) with f(c) = d
Key trig limits: lim_{x→0} sin(x)/x = 1 and lim_{x→0} (1−cos x)/x = 0
learning_objectives: Represent and interpret limits analytically using correct notation
Estimate limit values from graphs and tables using one-sided limits
Determine limits using algebraic manipulation and the Squeeze Theorem
Justify continuity at a point using the three-condition definition
Apply the Intermediate Value Theorem to prove existence of solutions
common_question_types: Evaluate a limit algebraically by factoring or simplifying
Determine whether a function is continuous at a given point and justify
Use IVT to argue that a solution exists on a given interval
Identify the type of discontinuity from a graph or equation
exam_tips: Always verify that a limit is in the form 0/0 or ∞/∞ before applying L'Hôpital's Rule — this rule does not apply otherwise.
When citing IVT on an FRQ, explicitly state that f is continuous on the interval before invoking the theorem.
Epsilon-delta definition of a limit is NOT assessed on the AB exam.

units.name: Differentiation: Definition and Fundamental Properties
key_topics: Derivative as limit of difference quotient
Power Rule, Constant Rule, Sum/Difference, Constant Multiple
Derivatives of sin x, cos x, e^x, ln x
Derivatives of tan x, cot x, sec x, csc x
key_formulas: Derivative definition: f'(x) = lim_{h→0} [f(x+h) − f(x)] / h
d/dx[sin x] = cos x; d/dx[cos x] = −sin x; d/dx[e^x] = e^x; d/dx[ln x] = 1/x
d/dx[tan x] = sec²x; d/dx[sec x] = sec x tan x; d/dx[csc x] = −csc x cot x; d/dx[cot x] = −csc²x
Tangent line: y − f(a) = f'(a)(x − a)
learning_objectives: Define the derivative as the limit of a difference quotient and interpret it as instantaneous rate of change
Determine when a function is differentiable and explain the connection between differentiability and continuity
Apply the Power, Product, and Quotient rules to compute derivatives
Differentiate trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions
Write the equation of a tangent line at a specified point
common_question_types: Compute a derivative using the limit definition
Apply Product or Quotient Rule to a combined function
Find the equation of a tangent line at a given x-value
Identify points where a function is not differentiable from a graph
exam_tips: Differentiability implies continuity, but continuity does NOT imply differentiability — a function can be continuous but have a corner or cusp where no derivative exists.
Memorize all six trig derivative pairs — they appear frequently in both multiple choice and FRQs.

units.name: Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, and Inverse Functions
key_topics: Derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions: arcsin, arccos, arctan
learning_objectives: Apply the Chain Rule to differentiate composite functions
Use implicit differentiation to find dy/dx when y is defined implicitly
Compute derivatives of inverse functions using the inverse function derivative formula
Differentiate arcsin, arccos, and arctan
Calculate second and higher-order derivatives
common_question_types: Differentiate a composite function using the Chain Rule
Find dy/dx or d²y/dx² using implicit differentiation
Evaluate the derivative of an inverse function at a given point
Compute a second derivative of a function involving trig or exponential terms
exam_tips: For implicit differentiation on FRQs, always apply the Chain Rule when differentiating terms involving y — write dy/dx explicitly at each step.
When evaluating (f⁻¹)'(x), you need f⁻¹(x) first; look for a given point where f(a) = x so that f⁻¹(x) = a, then compute 1/f'(a).

units.name: Contextual Applications of Differentiation
key_topics: Interpreting the derivative in context with correct units
Local linearity and tangent line approximation (linearization)
L'Hôpital's Rule for indeterminate forms 0/0 and ∞/∞
key_formulas: L'Hôpital's Rule: if lim f/g = 0/0 or ∞/∞, then lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x)
learning_objectives: Interpret the meaning of a derivative in applied context, including correct units
Relate position, velocity, acceleration, and speed for straight-line motion
Set up and solve related rates problems by differentiating an equation implicitly with respect to time
Use linearization to approximate function values near a known point
Apply L'Hôpital's Rule to evaluate indeterminate limits
common_question_types: Interpret the sign or value of v(t) or a(t) in a particle motion context
Set up a related rates equation and solve for an unknown rate
Use a tangent line approximation to estimate a function value
Evaluate an indeterminate limit using L'Hôpital's Rule
exam_tips: In related rates problems, draw a diagram, write an equation relating the quantities, then differentiate both sides with respect to t before substituting values.
L'Hôpital's Rule can only be applied when the limit is in the form 0/0 or ∞/∞ — verify this first.
Speed is |v(t)| not v(t); an object slows down when velocity and acceleration have opposite signs.

units.name: Analytical Applications of Differentiation
key_topics: Increasing and decreasing intervals using sign of f'
First Derivative Test for local extrema
Candidates Test (closed interval method) for absolute extrema
Concavity and inflection points using sign of f''
Second Derivative Test for local extrema
key_formulas: MVT: f'(c) = [f(b) − f(a)] / (b − a) for some c ∈ (a,b), given f continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b)
EVT: f continuous on [a,b] → f attains both an absolute max and min on [a,b]
Concave up: f''(x) > 0; Concave down: f''(x) < 0; Inflection point: f'' changes sign
Second Derivative Test: f'(c)=0 and f''(c)>0 → local min; f''(c)<0 → local max; f''(c)=0 → inconclusive
learning_objectives: Apply the Mean Value Theorem to justify the existence of a specific instantaneous rate of change
Use the Extreme Value Theorem to guarantee existence of absolute extrema on a closed interval
Determine intervals of increase/decrease and classify local extrema using the First Derivative Test
Find absolute extrema on a closed interval using the Candidates Test
Analyze concavity and locate inflection points; apply the Second Derivative Test
common_question_types: Determine intervals where f is increasing or decreasing from a graph or equation of f'
Find all absolute extrema on a closed interval using the Candidates Test
Classify a critical point as local max, min, or neither using the First or Second Derivative Test
Apply MVT to verify an instantaneous rate equals an average rate on an interval
exam_tips: On FRQs, always justify extrema conclusions in terms of the sign change of f' (First Derivative Test) or the sign of f'' (Second Derivative Test) — vague language earns no justification points.
MVT requires both continuity on [a,b] AND differentiability on (a,b) — state both conditions when citing it.
The Candidates Test requires evaluating f at every critical point in the open interval AND both endpoints — never skip endpoints.

units.name: Integration and Accumulation of Change
key_topics: Riemann sums: left, right, midpoint, and trapezoidal approximations
Definite integral as limit of Riemann sum
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 1 (accumulation function)
Basic antiderivative rules (power, trig, exponential, logarithmic)
key_formulas: FTC Part 1: d/dx[∫_a^x f(t) dt] = f(x)
FTC Part 2: ∫_a^b f(x) dx = F(b) − F(a) where F' = f
Power rule (integral): ∫x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C, n ≠ −1
∫e^x dx = e^x + C; ∫(1/x) dx = ln|x| + C
∫cos x dx = sin x + C; ∫sin x dx = −cos x + C; ∫sec²x dx = tan x + C
learning_objectives: Approximate definite integrals using left, right, midpoint, and trapezoidal Riemann sums
Apply FTC Part 1 to differentiate accumulation functions, including with Chain Rule
Evaluate definite and indefinite integrals using FTC Part 2 and basic antiderivative rules
Use u-substitution to evaluate integrals of composite functions
Interpret the definite integral as net accumulation of a rate of change
common_question_types: Differentiate an accumulation function of the form F(x) = ∫_a^{g(x)} f(t) dt using FTC Part 1
Evaluate a definite integral using FTC Part 2
Perform u-substitution to evaluate an integral
Determine whether a Riemann sum over- or under-approximates based on concavity or monotonicity
exam_tips: When differentiating ∫_a^{g(x)} f(t) dt, apply FTC Part 1 AND Chain Rule: the answer is f(g(x)) · g'(x).
Integration by parts, partial fractions, and improper integrals are BC-only — do not use them on AB.
Trapezoidal sums overestimate when the function is concave up and underestimate when concave down.

units.name: Differential Equations
key_topics: Writing differential equations from verbal descriptions
Separation of variables for general solutions
key_formulas: Exponential model: dy/dt = ky → y = Ce^(kt)
Separation of variables: rewrite as g(y) dy = f(x) dx, then integrate both sides
Particular solution: solve for C using the initial condition y(t₀) = y₀
Exponential growth: k > 0; Exponential decay: k < 0
learning_objectives: Translate verbal descriptions into differential equations
Verify that a given function is a solution to a differential equation
Sketch slope fields and use them to reason about solution behavior
Solve separable differential equations and state general solutions
Find particular solutions by applying initial conditions
common_question_types: Sketch or interpret a slope field for a given differential equation
Solve a separable ODE and find a particular solution with a given initial condition
Write a differential equation from a growth/decay scenario
Verify a proposed function is a solution by substituting into the equation
exam_tips: Euler's method and logistic models are BC-only — do not apply them on AB.
After separating variables, remember to include the constant of integration (+C) on one side before solving for it using the initial condition.
On FRQs involving slope fields, solutions must pass through the initial condition point and be tangent to the slope marks at each point along the curve.

units.name: Applications of Integration
key_topics: Average value of a function on an interval
Position, displacement, and total distance using integrals
Area between two curves (functions of x and of y)
Volumes with known cross sections (squares, semicircles, triangles)
key_formulas: Area between curves (x): ∫_a^b [f(x) − g(x)] dx, where f(x) ≥ g(x)
learning_objectives: Calculate the average value of a continuous function on a closed interval
Distinguish displacement from total distance and compute each using integration
Set up and evaluate area integrals for regions between two curves
Set up and evaluate volume integrals using the disc and washer methods
Set up volume integrals for solids with known cross-sectional shapes
common_question_types: Find the area of a region bounded by two curves, possibly requiring finding intersection points
Set up and compute the volume of a solid of revolution using the disc or washer method
Calculate displacement and total distance traveled given a velocity function
Find the average value of a function and interpret it contextually
exam_tips: Displacement is the net integral of velocity; total distance requires integrating |v(t)| — split the integral at zeros of v(t) where the particle changes direction.
For washers, the formula is π∫(R² − r²) dx where R is the outer radius and r is the inner radius — sketch the region to identify which curve is outer.
Arc length is a BC-only topic — it will not appear on the AB exam.
